annotations.label: Complete metric space

anchor.snippet: a metric space M is called complete

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `CompleteSpace` as a typeclass for uniform spaces where every Cauchy filter converges, generalizing the metric-space definition.

annotations.label: Rationals are not complete (intuitive)

anchor.snippet: the set of rational numbers is not complete

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain a direct statement that the rationals with the standard metric are not a CompleteSpace. The reals are constructed as the Cauchy completion of the rationals, which implicitly relies on this fact.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence in a metric space

anchor.snippet: is called Cauchy if for every positive real number

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `CauchySeq u` for sequences in uniform spaces. The metric-space epsilon-N characterization is proved as `cauchySeq_iff` in `Mathlib.Topology.MetricSpace.Cauchy`.

anchors.snippet: is called Cauchy if for every positive real number

annotations.label: Complete space (equivalent conditions)

anchor.snippet: is complete if any of the following equivalent conditions

annotations.note: The equivalence of the Cauchy-sequence and Cauchy-filter definitions follows from the general theory. `CompleteSpace` requires every Cauchy filter to converge.

annotations.label: Complete space — Cauchy sequence condition

anchor.snippet: Every Cauchy sequence in [MATH] converges in

annotations.note: The Cauchy sequence convergence condition is subsumed by the Cauchy filter formulation of `CompleteSpace`. The equivalence for metric spaces is available via `Metric.complete_of_cauchySeq_tendsto`.

annotations.label: Complete space — nested closed sets condition (Cantor's intersection theorem)

anchor.snippet: Every decreasing sequence of non-empty closed subsets

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Cantor's intersection theorem for compact spaces. For complete metric spaces with diameter tending to zero, the result follows from completeness. The compact version is `IsCompact.nonempty_iInter_of_sequence_nonempty_isCompact_isClosed`.

annotations.label: Rationals are not complete

anchor.snippet: of rational numbers, with the standard metric

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain a direct statement that the rationals with the standard metric are not a CompleteSpace. The reals are constructed as the Cauchy completion of the rationals (`Data.Real.Basic`), which implicitly relies on this fact.

annotations.label: Open interval (0,1) is not complete

anchor.snippet: The open interval (0,1) , again with the absolute difference metric, is not complete

annotations.note: There is no explicit Mathlib statement that the open interval (0,1) is not complete. This could be derived from facts about subspaces.

annotations.label: Closed interval [0,1] is complete

anchor.snippet: However the closed interval [0,1] is complete

annotations.note: The closed interval `Set.Icc 0 1` is a closed subset of the complete space R, so it inherits completeness via `IsClosed.isComplete`. Also `isCompact_Icc` gives compactness, which implies completeness.

annotations.label: Real numbers and complex numbers are complete

anchor.snippet: of real numbers and the space

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `Real.instCompleteSpace : CompleteSpace R` directly. Complex completeness is in `Mathlib.Analysis.Complex.Basic`.

annotations.label: Euclidean space is complete

anchor.snippet: and so is Euclidean space

annotations.note: Euclidean space `EuclideanSpace R (Fin n)` is a `PiLp` type. Completeness follows from `Pi.complete` (products of complete spaces are complete) and `CompleteSpace R`.

annotations.label: Banach space (complete normed vector space)

anchor.snippet: those that are complete are Banach spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a separate `BanachSpace` class. A Banach space is simply a `NormedSpace` that is also `CompleteSpace`. This convention is used throughout `Mathlib.Analysis.Normed`.

annotations.label: C([a,b]) is a Banach space with supremum norm

anchor.snippet: of continuous real-valued functions on a closed and bounded interval is a Banach space

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that bounded continuous functions `a ->b beta` form a `CompleteSpace` when the target is complete. Since [a,b] is compact, every continuous function on it is bounded, so this covers C([a,b]).

annotations.label: Supremum norm does not give a norm on C(R)

anchor.snippet: the supremum norm does not give a norm on the space

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain an explicit statement that the supremum norm fails to be a norm on C(R) due to unbounded functions. The `BoundedContinuousFunction` type sidesteps this by restricting to bounded functions.

annotations.label: Frechet space

anchor.snippet: a locally convex topological vector space whose topology can be induced by a complete translation-invariant metric

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define Frechet spaces as a class. While locally convex spaces and topological vector spaces exist in Mathlib, there is no dedicated Frechet space typeclass.

annotations.label: C(R) is a Frechet space with compact convergence topology

anchor.snippet: can be given the structure of a Fréchet space

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define Frechet spaces as a class. The topology of compact convergence on C(R) is not formalized in this specific way.

annotations.label: p-adic numbers are complete

anchor.snippet: of p -adic numbers is complete for any prime number

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `instance : CompleteSpace Q_[p]` in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.Padics.PadicNumbers`.

annotations.label: Sequence space over arbitrary set is complete

anchor.snippet: of all sequences in [MATH] becomes a complete metric space

annotations.note: The space of all sequences (functions N -> alpha, or more generally iota -> alpha) inherits completeness from `Pi.complete` when the target space is complete.

annotations.label: Geodesic manifold (complete Riemannian manifold)

anchor.snippet: Riemannian manifolds which are complete are called geodesic manifolds

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define geodesic manifolds or completeness for Riemannian manifolds. The differential geometry library does not yet reach this level.

annotations.label: Completeness of Riemannian manifolds follows from Hopf-Rinow

anchor.snippet: completeness follows from the Hopf–Rinow theorem

annotations.note: The Hopf-Rinow theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Every compact metric space is complete

anchor.snippet: Every compact metric space is complete

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `IsCompact.isComplete` for any uniform space: a compact set is complete. The global version follows via `isCompact_iff_totallyBounded_isComplete`.

annotations.label: A metric space is compact iff complete and totally bounded

anchor.snippet: a metric space is compact if and only if it is complete and totally bounded

annotations.note: Mathlib proves this for general uniform spaces: `isCompact_iff_totallyBounded_isComplete : IsCompact s <-> TotallyBounded s /\ IsComplete s`.

annotations.label: Heine-Borel theorem: closed and bounded subspace of R^n is compact

anchor.snippet: any closed and bounded subspace [MATH] of [MATH] is compact and therefore complete

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Heine-Borel via `ProperSpace`, where closed balls are compact. The instance for finite-dimensional normed spaces shows R^n is proper.

annotations.label: Closed subset of a complete space is complete

anchor.snippet: is a closed set, then [MATH] is also complete

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `IsClosed.isComplete` for uniform spaces: a closed subset of a complete space is complete.

annotations.label: Complete subspace of a metric space is closed

anchor.snippet: is a complete subspace, then [MATH] is also closed

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `IsComplete.isClosed` for T0 uniform spaces. Since metric spaces are T0, this gives that complete subspaces are closed.

annotations.label: Subspace of complete space is complete iff closed (theorem box)
annotations.note: The biconditional follows from combining `IsClosed.isComplete` (closed in complete implies complete) and `IsComplete.isClosed` (complete in T0 implies closed).

annotations.label: Bounded functions into complete space form a complete space

anchor.snippet: of all bounded functions [MATH] from [MATH] to [MATH] is a complete metric space

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that bounded continuous functions form a complete space when the target is complete. This is a special case (continuous bounded rather than all bounded), but the general result follows from `Pi.complete`.

anchors.snippet: of all bounded functions [MATH] from [MATH] to [MATH] is a complete metric space

annotations.label: Continuous bounded functions into complete space form closed subspace

anchor.snippet: consisting of all continuous bounded functions [MATH] is a closed subspace

annotations.note: Mathlib proves directly that bounded continuous functions `alpha ->b beta` form a `CompleteSpace` when the target is complete, which is equivalent to the continuous bounded functions being a closed subspace of bounded functions.

annotations.label: Baire category theorem: complete metric space is a Baire space

anchor.snippet: every complete metric space is a Baire space

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that completely pseudometrizable spaces are Baire in `BaireSpace.of_completelyPseudoMetrizable`. This generalizes the result from complete metric spaces to completely (pseudo)metrizable spaces.

annotations.label: Banach fixed-point theorem: contraction mapping admits a fixed point

anchor.snippet: a contraction mapping on a complete metric space admits a fixed point

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `ContractingWith K f` and provides `fixedPoint` giving the unique fixed point on a complete nonempty metric space, along with `fixedPoint_isFixedPt` and `fixedPoint_unique`.

annotations.label: Fixed-point theorem used to prove inverse function theorem

anchor.snippet: often used to prove the inverse function theorem on complete metric spaces such as Banach spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the inverse function theorem in `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.InverseFunctionTheorem.FDeriv` via `HasStrictFDerivAt.toOpenPartialHomeomorph`, using the Banach fixed-point theorem through `ApproximatesLinearOn`.

annotations.label: Ursescu's theorem (theorem box)
annotations.note: The Ursescu theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence of completion for any metric space

anchor.snippet: it is possible to construct a complete metric space

annotations.note: Mathlib constructs `UniformSpace.Completion alpha` for any uniform space and proves it is complete (`Completion.completeSpace`). For metric spaces, a compatible `MetricSpace` instance is given in `Mathlib.Topology.MetricSpace.Completion`.

annotations.label: Universal property of completion

anchor.snippet: there exists a unique uniformly continuous function

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `Completion.extension f` extending any uniformly continuous function to the completion, and `Completion.extension_unique` proving uniqueness.

annotations.label: Completion is unique up to isometry

anchor.snippet: is determined up to isometry by this property

annotations.note: Mathlib proves uniqueness via `AbstractCompletion.compareEquiv` which gives a uniform equivalence between any two abstract completions.

annotations.label: Completion constructed via equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences

anchor.snippet: can be constructed as a set of equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences

annotations.note: Mathlib constructs the completion as `SeparationQuotient (CauchyFilter alpha)`, using Cauchy filters rather than sequences. This is the generalization to uniform spaces of the equivalence-class-of-Cauchy-sequences construction.

anchors.snippet: can be constructed as a set of equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences

annotations.label: Distance on Cauchy sequences is a pseudometric

anchor.snippet: This is only a pseudometric , not yet a metric

annotations.note: Mathlib first equips the completion with a `PseudoMetricSpace` structure, then derives a `MetricSpace` via the separation quotient.

annotations.label: Embedding into completion defines an isometry onto dense subspace

anchor.snippet: This defines an isometry onto a dense subspace

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `Completion.coe_isometry : Isometry (coe : alpha -> Completion alpha)` and `Completion.denseRange_coe : DenseRange (coe : alpha -> Completion alpha)`.

annotations.label: Real numbers are completion of rationals

anchor.snippet: the real numbers are the completion of the rational numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib defines R as a structure wrapping `CauSeq.Completion.Cauchy` of Q with the absolute value, and `Real.equivCauchy` gives the equivalence. The construction is directly via Cauchy sequences of rationals.

annotations.label: Real numbers are the unique totally ordered complete field

anchor.snippet: is the unique totally ordered complete field

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain a formal uniqueness statement characterizing the reals as the unique totally ordered complete field (up to isomorphism).

annotations.label: p-adic numbers arise from completing rationals with different metric

anchor.snippet: the p -adic numbers arise by completing the rational numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Q_[p] as the Cauchy completion of Q with respect to the p-adic norm.

annotations.label: Completion of normed vector space is a Banach space

anchor.snippet: applied to a normed vector space, the result is a Banach space

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the completion of a normed space is again a normed space (`NormedSpace` instance on `Completion E`) and it is always `CompleteSpace`, giving a Banach space.

annotations.label: Completion of inner product space is a Hilbert space

anchor.snippet: applied to an inner product space , the result is a Hilbert space

annotations.note: Mathlib proves an `InnerProductSpace` instance on `Completion E` when `E` is an inner product space, and the completion is complete, giving a Hilbert space.

annotations.label: Completeness is a metric property, not topological

anchor.snippet: Completeness is a property of the metric and not of the topology

annotations.note: Mathlib distinguishes `CompleteSpace` (a property of the uniform structure) from `IsCompletelyMetrizableSpace` (a topological property: existence of some complete compatible metric). This distinction embodies the fact that completeness depends on the metric.

annotations.label: Reals are homeomorphic to (0,1) but complete vs not

anchor.snippet: complete but homeomorphic to the open interval (0,1) , which is not complete

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain the explicit example that R is homeomorphic to (0,1) as a witness to completeness being metric-dependent.

annotations.label: Completely metrizable space

anchor.snippet: completely metrizable spaces , spaces for which there exists at least one complete metric

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `IsCompletelyMetrizableSpace X` as a typeclass asserting that there exists a `MetricSpace` structure compatible with the topology making the space complete.

annotations.label: Characterization of completely metrizable spaces as G-delta sets

anchor.snippet: can be written as an intersection of countably many open subsets

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that open subsets of Polish spaces are Polish (`IsOpen.polishSpace`) and closed subsets are Polish (`IsClosed.polishSpace`). The full Alexandrov theorem (G-delta subset of complete metric space is completely metrizable) is partially covered but not stated in full generality as a biconditional.

annotations.label: Baire category theorem applies to completely metrizable spaces

anchor.snippet: Since the conclusion of the Baire category theorem is purely topological, it applies to these spaces as well

annotations.note: Mathlib directly proves that completely pseudometrizable spaces are Baire spaces, which is exactly this statement.

annotations.label: Completely uniformizable space

anchor.snippet: the term topologically complete for a wider class of topological spaces, the completely uniformizable spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define 'completely uniformizable space' as a class. The file `Mathlib.Topology.UniformSpace.Uniformizable` discusses uniformizable spaces but not the complete variant.

annotations.label: Polish space

anchor.snippet: homeomorphic to a separable complete metric space is called a Polish space

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `PolishSpace alpha` as a typeclass for separable completely metrizable spaces.

annotations.label: Completeness via group structure (topological groups)

anchor.snippet: an alternative to relying on a metric structure for defining completeness

annotations.note: Mathlib defines left and right uniform structures on topological groups and proves that completeness with respect to both agrees. This is the formalization of completeness via group structure.

annotations.label: Completeness in uniform spaces

anchor.snippet: A common generalization of these definitions can be found in the context of a uniform space

annotations.note: Mathlib's `CompleteSpace` is defined for uniform spaces, which is precisely the common generalization described in the article.

annotations.label: Completeness via Cauchy nets or Cauchy filters

anchor.snippet: replace Cauchy sequences in the definition of completeness by Cauchy nets or Cauchy filters

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Cauchy f` for a filter `f` in a uniform space. Filters generalize both nets and sequences; this is the primary formalization of the Cauchy concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complete space via Cauchy nets/filters

anchor.snippet: If every Cauchy net (or equivalently every Cauchy filter) has a limit

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `CompleteSpace alpha` exactly as requiring every Cauchy filter to converge, which is the filter-based generalization described in this article section.

annotations.label: Completion exists for arbitrary uniform spaces

anchor.snippet: construct a completion for an arbitrary uniform space similar to the completion of metric spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib constructs `UniformSpace.Completion alpha` for any uniform space and proves it is a complete Hausdorff uniform space with a dense uniform embedding from alpha.

annotations.label: Cauchy spaces as most general setting for Cauchy nets

anchor.snippet: The most general situation in which Cauchy nets apply is Cauchy spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define Cauchy spaces (in the sense of axiomatic Cauchy structures). This is a concept from general topology not yet formalized.
